title: French Drain Installation

budget.description: Exterior French drain, shallow trench (18 inches), corrugated perforated pipe, landscape fabric, gravel backfill, about 50 linear feet

midRange.description: Interior perimeter French drain, 100 linear feet along basement walls, rigid PVC pipe, connected to sump pit, concrete cutting and patching

premium.description: Full interior and exterior French drain system, 150+ linear feet, commercial-grade drainage board, multiple drain connections, sump pump integration, full waterproofing membrane

costFactors.title: Interior vs Exterior Installation
costFactors.description: Interior French drains typically cost $40-$75 per linear foot because they require cutting through a concrete basement floor, excavating underneath, and patching the concrete afterward. Exterior French drains run $25-$50 per linear foot and involve trenching along the foundation, which requires heavy equipment and landscaping restoration. Interior drains are more common for existing homes because exterior excavation is more disruptive.

costFactors.title: Length of Drain Run
costFactors.description: A short 30-foot drain along one wall might cost $1,200-$2,500. A full perimeter drain (100-150 linear feet for an average basement) runs $4,000-$10,000. Exterior drains around the full foundation perimeter of a 1,500 sq ft home can push $6,000-$12,000 when you factor in equipment, excavation depth, and landscape repair.

costFactors.title: Depth of Excavation
costFactors.description: Exterior French drains need to be deep enough to intercept groundwater before it reaches the foundation. Typical depth is 18-24 inches for yard drainage but 4-6 feet for foundation drainage. Every additional foot of depth increases labor and equipment costs significantly. Rocky soil or high water tables add further complications.

costFactors.title: Sump Pump Connection
costFactors.description: Interior French drains need somewhere to send the collected water. If you already have a sump pump and pit, connection costs $200-$400. If you need a new sump pit and pump, add $600-$1,500 to the project. Most interior French drain installations include sump pump setup since the drain is useless without a way to remove the collected water.

costFactors.title: Soil Conditions and Access
costFactors.description: Clay soil is harder to excavate and drains poorly, which may require wider trenches and more gravel. Rocky soil requires specialized equipment. Tight access around the home's exterior can prevent heavy equipment from reaching work areas, forcing more hand digging. Interior drains in basements with finished floors require demolition and restoration of flooring.

materialOptions.pros: Cheapest option, flexible and easy to install, widely available, good for simple exterior drains
materialOptions.cons: Can crush under weight, corrugations trap sediment over time, shorter lifespan (10-15 years)
materialOptions.bestFor: Budget exterior yard drainage projects, temporary solutions, shallow installations

materialOptions.pros: Very durable (50+ year lifespan), smooth interior resists clogging, handles high water volume, maintains shape
materialOptions.cons: More expensive, requires fittings at every turn, rigid so it can't bend around obstacles
materialOptions.bestFor: Interior basement French drains, deep exterior foundation drains, permanent installations

materialOptions.pros: Built-in filter prevents sediment infiltration, reduces maintenance, good lifespan
materialOptions.cons: Filter sock can clog in very silty soil, moderate cost, not as strong as rigid PVC
materialOptions.bestFor: Exterior drains in sandy or loamy soil, mid-budget projects where low maintenance matters

materialOptions.pros: Pipe pre-wrapped in gravel aggregate, no separate gravel needed, faster installation, consistent drainage
materialOptions.cons: More expensive per foot, limited sizes, heavier to handle, not available everywhere
materialOptions.bestFor: DIY exterior projects, tight trenches where gravel is hard to place, projects where speed matters

materialOptions.costPerUnit: $2 - $5 per sq ft of wall coverage
materialOptions.pros: Creates air gap against foundation wall, channels water down to drain, protects waterproofing membrane
materialOptions.cons: Only used with interior or exterior wall drainage, adds significant cost, requires professional installation
materialOptions.bestFor: Comprehensive interior waterproofing systems, used alongside French drains for maximum protection

northeast.label: Northeast
northeast.note: High water tables and older homes with stone foundations create strong demand. Rocky soil in New England adds excavation costs.

westCoast.label: West Coast
westCoast.note: High labor rates drive costs up. Hillside properties in California often need extensive exterior drainage systems.

southeast.label: Southeast
southeast.note: Lower labor costs but high water tables and clay soil are common, sometimes requiring more extensive drainage solutions.

midwest.label: Midwest
midwest.note: Very high demand for French drains due to spring snowmelt and flat terrain that creates poor natural drainage. Competitive contractor pricing.

mountainWest.label: Mountain West
mountainWest.note: Snowmelt creates seasonal drainage issues. Rocky soil in mountain communities can increase excavation costs.

phases.name: Sump pit and pump connection (if applicable)
Backfill, concrete patching, or landscape restoration

diyVsPro.diyFeasibility: Low to Moderate - Simple exterior yard drains at shallow depths are a reasonable DIY project for physically fit homeowners. Interior basement French drains require concrete cutting, significant excavation, and plumbing knowledge that's beyond most DIYers. Foundation-level exterior drains involve deep excavation that's both dangerous and requires heavy equipment.
diyVsPro.diySavings: 40-60% for exterior yard drains, not recommended for interior foundation drains
diyTasks: Shallow exterior yard drainage (18-24 inch depth)
Dig trenches in soft soil by hand for short runs (under 40 feet)
Lay pipe, landscape fabric, and gravel in excavated trenches
Connect discharge to existing drainage or daylight outlet
proOnlyTasks: Interior basement French drain with concrete cutting
Deep exterior foundation drainage (4-6 feet deep)
Sump pump pit installation and connection
Waterproofing membrane application on foundation walls
Any work near utility lines or structural foundations
diyVsPro.diyRisks: The biggest risk with DIY French drains is improper slope. The drain must maintain a minimum slope of 1% (1 inch per 8 feet) to function. Flat or back-graded sections create standing water in the pipe and eventual failure. Another serious risk is digging near buried utilities. Always call 811 before any excavation. For interior drains, improper concrete cutting can damage the foundation footing.

savingsTips: Get quotes for both interior and exterior solutions. Interior drains are often less expensive because they don't require heavy excavation equipment or landscape restoration.
If you only have water issues on one wall, a partial drain (30-50 linear feet) costs far less than a full perimeter system and may solve your problem entirely.
Combine your French drain with sump pump installation as one project. Bundling saves $200-$500 versus hiring separate contractors.
Schedule the work during dry months (late summer or fall) when contractors are less busy and the water table is lowest, making excavation easier.
For exterior drains, DIY the landscape restoration yourself. Regrading, reseeding, and replanting after the contractor finishes the drain work saves $300-$800.
Ask about rigid PVC pipe instead of corrugated. It costs slightly more upfront but lasts 3-4 times longer and resists clogging, saving you from expensive redo work.
Check with your city about stormwater rebates. Some municipalities offer $500-$2,000 in rebates for drainage improvements that reduce runoff into public systems.
Get the drain cleaned every 3-5 years ($150-$300) rather than waiting until it clogs and needs replacement ($3,000-$8,000).

contractorQuestions.question: Do you recommend an interior or exterior French drain for my situation, and why?
contractorQuestions.why: The right approach depends on where water is entering. A contractor who evaluates your specific water intrusion pattern before recommending a solution is more trustworthy than one who defaults to the same approach for every home.

contractorQuestions.question: What pipe material will you use, and what's the expected lifespan?
contractorQuestions.why: Corrugated pipe is cheaper but clogs more easily and lasts 10-15 years. Rigid PVC costs more but lasts 50+ years. Knowing what you're getting helps you assess the true long-term value.

contractorQuestions.question: How will you ensure proper slope throughout the drain run?
contractorQuestions.why: The drain must slope at least 1% toward the collection point. A professional should use a laser level to verify grade. Improper slope is the number one reason French drains fail.

contractorQuestions.question: Is a sump pump included, and what happens to the collected water?
contractorQuestions.why: Water needs somewhere to go. If the drain can't gravity-flow to daylight, you need a sump pump. Make sure the quote addresses the full water removal path, not just the drain pipe.

contractorQuestions.question: What warranty do you offer, and does it cover drain failure?
contractorQuestions.why: A quality French drain should last 15-30 years. Look for at least a 5-year warranty on workmanship. Some waterproofing companies offer transferable lifetime warranties, which adds real value at resale.

contractorQuestions.question: Do I need permits for this work, and who handles that?
contractorQuestions.why: Many municipalities require permits for drainage work, especially if it connects to storm sewers or affects neighboring properties. The contractor should handle permits and ensure the installation meets local codes.

contractorQuestions.question: Will this solve my water problem completely, or is additional waterproofing needed?
contractorQuestions.why: French drains address water at the floor level, but wall seepage may require additional waterproofing treatments. An honest contractor will tell you upfront if the drain alone won't fully solve your issue.

secondaryKeywords: how much does a french drain cost
french drain cost per linear foot

seo.metaDescription: French drain installation costs $2,500-$10,000 for most homes in 2026. Compare interior vs exterior options, materials, and labor costs with our detailed guide and calculator.

sources.name: Angi - How Much Does a French Drain Cost?
